reasoning: The 3-month treasury yield trended aggressively downward in Fall 2025 as the Fed cut rates, but has stabilized essentially flat at roughly 3.65% since mid-December. The FRED statistical tool outputs a near-term P(increase) of 0.46 as its combined anchor. Web search heavily corroborates this stabilization: markets have aggressively pared back expectations for a January 2026 Fed rate cut (from ~25% down to 15%), and the Fed's December 'dot plot' signaled only one more cut may occur in all of 2026. Furthermore, headlines from early January report a 'yield shock' and Treasury sell-off due to fiscal/inflation uncertainty ahead of the incoming administration. The pricing-out of rate cuts suggests the short-term yield will stop aggressively dropping, and upward shocks are entirely plausible. Given the flat recent movement and upcoming Fed hold, the chance of an increase relative to the Jan 4 baseline is just slightly below 50% in the near term (Jan 11), migrating closer to a coin toss for the longer horizons (Feb 3, Apr 4) reflecting rising forward uncertainty.
